risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Following consent, participants were randomly assigned to condition using a computerized blocked randomization method." Comment: This explicitly describes a computer-based random allocation process with blocking, which is an appropriate random sequence generation method.

Quote: "Following consent, participants were randomly assigned to condition using a computerized blocked randomization method." Comment: This describes the method used to generate the allocation sequence, but it does not state how the upcoming assignment was concealed from the person enrolling participants. There is no explicit mention of central randomization, sequentially numbered opaque sealed envelopes, pharmacy control, or another concealment mechanism preventing foreknowledge.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "The current pilot randomized controlled trial aimed to replicate the feasibility of RRFT and to evaluate the differential efficacy of RRFT and treatment as usual (TAU)..." and "RRFT was developed to reduce risk of SU and other high-risk behaviors and trauma-related psychopathology..." and "TAU was selected as the control condition... no one treatment emerged as being consistently delivered across youth and families assigned to this condition." Comment: This was a psychotherapy trial comparing a named, structured family therapy (RRFT) with treatment as usual, so participants and treating personnel would necessarily know which intervention was being delivered; no blinding of participants or therapists is described. The main outcomes were largely subjective and potentially influenced by knowledge of allocation (e.g., PTSD, depression, internalizing/externalizing symptoms, family cohesion/conflict, self-reported substance use, sexual behavior). Therefore lack of blinding could plausibly affect outcomes, giving high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "PTSD symptoms were assessed with the UCLA PTSD Index for DSM-IV-Adolescent & Caregiver versions... The Child Depression Inventory (CDI) was used to assess depression symptoms and the Behavioral Assessment System for Children (BASC-2) (parent and youth self-report) measured participants’ internalizing and externalizing symptoms." Quote: "The Time Line Follow Back Interview (TLFB)... was conducted with each participant... The Cohesion and Conflict subscales of the Family Environment Scale (FES) were completed by adolescents and caregivers." Comment: The outcome assessors for the most vulnerable outcomes were the patients/caregivers themselves (and interview-based self-report), not blinded independent assessors. This psychotherapy trial compared RRFT with TAU, so participants/caregivers would necessarily know which treatment they were receiving. Because the study includes multiple subjective self-reported outcomes (PTSD, depression, internalizing/externalizing symptoms, substance use, family functioning, sexual behavior), knowledge of allocation could influence outcome measurement. By the worst-outcome rule, the presence of these unblinded subjective outcomes makes detection bias High risk overall, even though urine drug screens were also collected.

Quote: "Participants included 30 treatment seeking adolescent CSA victims... Assessment measures were re-administered at post-treatment, 3-, and 6-month follow-up..." and "Treatment completers were defined as having completed 5 of 7 RRFT components. All but two RRFT participants met this criterion." and "Data were comprised of four repeated measurements (level-1) nested within 30 families (level-2), yielding a two-level Mixed-Effects Regression Model (MRM)." Comment: The paper reports the randomized sample and notes two RRFT treatment non-completers, but it does not report per-arm numbers analysed for the outcomes at post-treatment, 3-month, or 6-month follow-up, nor reasons for missing outcome data by arm. Treatment completion is not the same as outcome assessment completeness. Although mixed-effects models were used, the article does not provide enough outcome-specific attrition information to judge whether missing data were low/balanced or likely to bias results.
